items.q: What did Magna Carta actually do in 1215?
items.correct: It was a peace treaty between King John and rebel barons protecting baronial privileges and limiting arbitrary royal taxation and imprisonment — it was annulled within months.
items.misconception: Magna Carta gave ordinary English people democratic rights and freedom.
items.random_wrong: Magna Carta abolished the monarchy and created Parliament.
items.why: Magna Carta protected the elite, not commoners; villeins are barely mentioned. Its democratic reputation is a later (17th-century) reinterpretation projected backwards.

items.q: What were the long-term effects of the Black Death?
items.correct: Labour shortages strengthened peasants' bargaining power, weakened serfdom in England, contributed to the Peasants' Revolt, and reshaped religious authority.
items.misconception: The Black Death wiped out medieval society and ended the Middle Ages overnight.
items.random_wrong: The Black Death was caused by witches and was stopped by burning them.
items.why: The plague was catastrophic but medieval society continued. Its importance lies in slow structural changes (wages, labour, religion) — not in being a single 'ending' event.

items.q: Why did William of Normandy claim the English throne in 1066?
items.correct: He claimed Edward the Confessor had promised him the throne in 1051 and that Harold Godwinson had sworn an oath to support that claim in 1064.
items.misconception: William invaded because he was the strongest king in Europe and wanted more land.
items.random_wrong: William was Edward the Confessor's eldest son.
items.why: Students treat the invasion as raw conquest, missing that William framed it as enforcing a legitimate hereditary and oath-based claim — legitimacy mattered hugely in 11th-century politics.

items.q: Which event in 1588 helped secure Elizabeth I's reputation?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Defeat of the Spanish Armada. “Battle of Bosworth” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.correct: Things staying the same over time
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Things staying the same over time. “A sudden revolt” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In which year was the Glorious Revolution?
items.why: Exact answer: 1688. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
